html:
In recent years, organized crime has increasingly impacted various aspects of society. Serious crimes committed by young people are particularly concerning and are occurring at younger ages. To ensure that legislation addresses the needs arising from this trend in criminal activity, and to maintain the credibility and legitimacy of the penal system, the rules for juvenile offenders need to be strengthened. Therefore, the government proposes the following measures:

  • The age of criminal responsibility will be lowered to 14 years for serious crimes, for a period of five years.
  • The youth discount will be completely abolished for young adults.
  • The youth discount will be reduced, and the maximum prison sentence will be increased to 18 years for offenders under 18 years old.
  • The supervision measure for juveniles will be strengthened.
  • The regulations regarding breaches of probation orders for juvenile care and community service will be tightened.

These legislative changes are proposed to come into effect on September 10, 2026.

Proposed Resolutions for the Parliament

Prop. 2025/26:293

Government Proposal:

  1. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Penal Code.
  2. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Penal Code.
  3. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Social Security Code.
  4. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (1964:167) on special provisions regarding young offenders.
  5. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (1964:167) on special provisions regarding young offenders.
  6. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Law (1971:69) on tax offenses.
  7. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (1978:801) on international cooperation concerning criminal rehabilitation in a free setting.

  12. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2003:1156) on extradition from Sweden pursuant to a European arrest warrant.
  13. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2007:979) on measures to prevent certain particularly serious crimes.
  14. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2007:979) on measures to prevent certain particularly serious crimes.
  15. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2009:1427) on recognition and enforcement of fines within the European Union.
  16. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Detention Act (2010:611).
  17. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2011:1165) on extradition from Sweden pursuant to a Nordic arrest warrant.
  18. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2012:278) on the collection of information about electronic communications in the intelligence activities of law enforcement authorities.
  19. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2012:278) on the collection of information about electronic communications in the intelligence activities of law enforcement authorities.
  20. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2015:96) on recognition and enforcement of deprivation of liberty within the European Union.
  21. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2015:485) on recognition and follow-up of decisions regarding surveillance measures within the European Union.
  1. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2015:650) on recognition and enforcement of probationary sentences within the European Union.
  2. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2018:1693) on the processing of personal data by the police within the scope of criminal data laws.
  3. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2018:1693) on the processing of personal data by the police within the scope of criminal data laws.

  5. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2020:62) on secret data interception.

  8. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Customs Powers Act (2024:710).

  10. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendments to the Act (2025:520) on international enforcement in criminal cases.

If a person has committed an offense before reaching the age of eighteen, their youth must be taken into account to a reasonable extent when determining the sentence. In this assessment, consideration should be given to the offender's age at the time of the offense and the type of crime. The court may then impose a less severe penalty than prescribed for the offense.
tldr: The government wants to tighten the rules for young people who commit serious crimes. This includes making the penalties harsher and removing certain age-related reductions in sentences.
lattlast:
The government has proposed new laws to address the increasing crime rate among young people. Starting September 10, 2026, stricter penalties will be imposed on individuals under 18 who commit serious crimes. A key change is that the age of criminal responsibility, meaning the age at which a person can be convicted of a crime, will be lowered to 14 years for serious offenses, but only for a period of five years.

For young people over 18 who are still considered "young" under the law, the so-called youth discount will be completely eliminated. This means they will no longer receive reduced sentences simply because of their age. For those under 18, the reduction in sentence will decrease, and the maximum possible prison sentence will be increased to 18 years.

In addition to harsher penalties, the monitoring of young offenders will also become stricter. This means that individuals subject to youth supervision will be monitored more closely. The purpose of all these changes is to make the justice system more credible and clear when it comes to dealing with juvenile crime.
